title: Quiz: Von Neumann Entropy
description: Test your understanding of von Neumann entropy and quantum information measures

questions.question: What is the von Neumann entropy of a pure state |ψ⟩?
questions.explanation: A pure state has ρ = |ψ⟩⟨ψ| with eigenvalues {1, 0, 0, ...}. Therefore S = -1·log(1) - 0·log(0)... = 0. Pure states have no uncertainty.

questions.question: For a qubit maximally mixed state ρ = I/2, the von Neumann entropy is:
How does unitary evolution U affect the entropy of a state ρ?
questions.explanation: S(UρU†) = S(ρ). Unitary operations preserve the eigenvalues of ρ, and entropy depends only on eigenvalues. This is why quantum gates don't increase entropy.

questions.question: The state |+⟩ = (|0⟩ + |1⟩)/√2 has entropy 1 bit because it's a superposition of two states.
questions.explanation: False. |+⟩ is a pure state with S = 0. The entropy measures uncertainty about the quantum state, not measurement outcomes. We know exactly that the state is |+⟩.

questions.question: For a Bell state |Φ+⟩ = (|00⟩ + |11⟩)/√2, what is S(ρ_A) for the reduced state of qubit A?
questions.explanation: The reduced state ρ_A = Tr_B(|Φ+⟩⟨Φ+|) = I/2, maximally mixed. S(ρ_A) = 1 bit. This measures the entanglement - Bell states are maximally entangled.

questions.question: The von Neumann entropy S(ρ) equals the Shannon entropy of:

options.text: The measurement probabilities in any basis

questions.explanation: S(ρ) = -Tr(ρ log ρ) = -Σᵢ λᵢ log λᵢ = H({λᵢ}), where {λᵢ} are the eigenvalues. The entropy is basis-independent.

questions.question: For a bipartite pure state |ψ_AB⟩, we always have S(ρ_A) = S(ρ_B).
questions.explanation: True. This is a fundamental property: for pure bipartite states, both reduced density matrices have the same non-zero eigenvalues (Schmidt decomposition), so their entropies are equal.

questions.question: Which properties does von Neumann entropy satisfy? (Select all)
questions.explanation: a) Yes, S ≥ 0 always. b) Yes, for product states. c) Yes, subadditivity holds. d) No, entropy is CONCAVE, not convex: S(Σpᵢρᵢ) ≥ Σpᵢ S(ρᵢ).
